coredata.dc:title: Enhancement in hydrogen sorption behaviour of MgH2 catalyzed by graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Kesarwani, Rashmi
Bhatnagar, Ashish
Verma, Satish K.
Hudson, M. Sterlin Leo
Shaz, M.A.
coredata.dc:description: The present investigation reports the synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by the microwave-assisted green synthesis method, and its catalytic effect in improving the hydrogen storage behaviour of magnesium hydride (MgH2). Transmission electron microscopy, UV–Vis, Raman, FTIR and XRD analysis was done to characterize the samples. It has been observed that the as-synthesized GQDs are in the size range of 3–12 nm. The catalytic activity of GQDs on improving the de/re-hydrogenation kinetics of MgH2 has been investigated with different additive concentrations. It has been observed that there is an improvement in the hydrogen sorption characteristics of ball-milled MgH2 when 7 wt.% GQDs was employed. The onset dehydrogenation temperature of 7 wt.% GQDs admixed ball milled MgH2 (7 % GQDs-MgH2) was observed at 300 ᵒC, which is 60 ᵒC lower than that of additive free ball-milled MgH2 under identical condition. Furthermore, the 7 % GQDs-MgH2 sample reabsorbs nearly 5 wt.% hydrogen in 2.5 min at 300 ᵒC and 15 atm hydrogen pressure. The GQDs catalyzed MgH2 sample exhibit good de/re-hydrogenation cyclic stability. No significant loss in hydrogen capacity was noticed even after 25 de-/re-hydrogenation cycles. From the Van't Hoff plot, the formation enthalpy of 7 % GQDs-MgH2 was estimated to be 58 kJmol-1.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnesium hydride
Hydrogenation kinetics
Hydrogen storage
Metal hydrides

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural and microstructural characterization of GQDs
Hydrogen storage analysis of MgH2 catalyzed by GQD
Tentative catalytic mechanism of GQDs in MgH2

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial support from Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) , New Delhi, India under the Mission Mode Hydrogen Storage Project Scheme, Department of Science and Technology , New Delhi,India and National Academy of Sciences India (NASI) , are acknowledged. Thanks are also due to Late Prof. O. N. Sirvastava for his guidence and suggestions. Thanks to Prof. R. S. Tiwari for helpful discussions. The authors MAS and AB would like to acknowledge CRG-SERB ( CRG/2023/003055 ) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of synthesis protocol adopted for GQDs synthesis.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra (b) FTIR Spectrum of as-synthesized GQDs (c) X-ray diffractogram (d) Raman Spectra of as-synthesized GQDs (e) TEM image of as synthesized GQDs (f) HR-TEM image of GQD particle(g) SAED pattern of as synthesized GQD (h & i) SEM of as synthesized GQD at 10 μm (i)SEM of as synthesized GQD at 4 μm (j) AFM image of as synthesized GQD at pH 7 (k) 3D AFM image of as synthesized GQD and (l) corresponding height profile of AFM image.
Comparative TPD profile of MgH2 ball-milled with and without additive.
Ball milled MgH2 with 7 wt.% GQDs as catalyst additive (i) Dehydrogenation kinetics at 300 ᵒC and 1 atm H2 (ii) Re-hydrogenation kinetics of dehydrogenated sample at 300 °C and 15 atm H2.
De-/re-hydrogenation cyclic stability curves of ball milled MgH2 with 7 wt.% GQDs as a catalyst additive.
(a) The PCI absorption of ball milled MgH2 with 7 wt.% GQDs as catalyst additive (b) corresponding van't Hoff plot.
Schematic diagram showing de-/re hydrogenation of MgH2@GQD.
FTIR Peak Spectrum OF MGQD synthesis from mango leaves.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The FTIR data signifies the presence of various characteristic group like CC,C–H and O–H in graphene quantum dots.

head.ce:title: Enhancement in hydrogen sorption behaviour of MgH2 catalyzed by graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hydrogen Energy Centre, Department of Physics, Banaras Hindu University, Varanasi, 221005, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics and Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics and Materials Science and Engineering, Jaypee Institute of Information Technology, Noida, 201309, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present investigation reports the synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by the microwave-assisted green synthesis method, and its catalytic effect in improving the hydrogen storage behaviour of magnesium hydride (MgH2). Transmission electron microscopy, UV–Vis, Raman, FTIR and XRD analysis was done to characterize the samples. It has been observed that the as-synthesized GQDs are in the size range of 3–12 nm. The catalytic activity of GQDs on improving the de/re-hydrogenation kinetics of MgH2 has been investigated with different additive concentrations. It has been observed that there is an improvement in the hydrogen sorption characteristics of ball-milled MgH2 when 7 wt.% GQDs was employed. The onset dehydrogenation temperature of 7 wt.% GQDs admixed ball milled MgH2 (7 % GQDs-MgH2) was observed at 300 ᵒC, which is 60 ᵒC lower than that of additive free ball-milled MgH2 under identical condition. Furthermore, the 7 % GQDs-MgH2 sample reabsorbs nearly 5 wt.% hydrogen in 2.5 min at 300 ᵒC and 15 atm hydrogen pressure. The GQDs catalyzed MgH2 sample exhibit good de/re-hydrogenation cyclic stability. No significant loss in hydrogen capacity was noticed even after 25 de-/re-hydrogenation cycles. From the Van't Hoff plot, the formation enthalpy of 7 % GQDs-MgH2 was estimated to be 58 kJmol-1.

ce:section.ce:para: As the world's population increases, the disparity between energy supply and demand has also widened significantly in the recent decades. This causes raising concern over future energy security. Fossil fuels are currently satisfying 85 % of the global energy needs but they are neither renewable nor environmentally friendly [1]. The usage of fossil fuel causes serious environmental damage due to greenhouse gas emissions. Moreover, the damage already done to the environment and human health from fossil fuel emissions are irrevocable. If the fossil fuel usage rate is not controlled, then it is expected that the overall global temperature may increase at an alarmingly high limit beyond human survival and the earth will soon become uninhabitable [2]. Hence, clean and renewable energy sources or carriers are required to address the energy demand. The most appealing one is hydrogen. Hydrogen forms a clean and closed energy cycle as it can be produced from water and after usage, the only by-product emission is water [3,4]. Recently, hydrogen energy has gained significant interest and tremendous amount of research activities are being focused on green hydrogen production, solid state hydrogen storage and fuel cell applications. However, Hydrogen storage is considered to be a crucial issue, which bridges the production and utilization. Moreover, it also addresses the safety of hydrogen energy applications [5,6].

Compared to risky high-compression gas tanks and expensive cryogenic liquid storage, solid-state hydrogen storage in the form of metal hydrides materials offers efficient and safe mode of hydrogen storage, it also exhibits facile temperature manipulation and low working pressure [7–10]. The US-DoE gravimetric and volumetric hydrogen storage system target for year 2025 is 5.5 wt.% H2 and 40 gH2L−1, respectively [11]. Materials having a hydrogen storage capacity of more than 5.5 wt.% H2 and having 40 gH2L−1 may accomplish such a system target. MgH2, a reversible hydrogen storage material having gravimetric and volumetric hydrogen density of 7.6 wt.% H2 and 110 gH2L−1, respectively has been considered as a potential candidate for hydrogen storage applications [12–14]. However, MgH2 faces two challenges: high enthalpy of dissociation (∼76 kJmol−1) and sluggish hydrogen desorption and absorption kinetics (<1 wt.% min−1). Several studies have been undertaken to overcome these challenges, which include nanoscaling, catalyst additives and alloying. In these attempts, catalyst additives (more specifically the size and distribution of catalyst) have shown to be the most effective means of improving the hydrogen sorption kinetics and de/re hydrogenation temperature of MgH2 [15–21].

Earlier studies suggest that ball-milling of MgH2 with catalyst additive is the most feasible way to improve the de/re-hydrogenation behaviour of MgH2 [20]. Sadhasivam et al. [22] described the dimensional effect of nanosized rare-earth metal alloys for improving the hydrogen storage behaviour of Mg/MgH2. Liuting et al. [23] employed TiH2 nanoparticles as catalyst/additives for MgH2 and found much improve hydrogen storage characteristics of MgH2. Apart from transition and rare earth metal-catalysts, recently, carbon-based materials were also demonstrated to have catalytic effect on improving the hydrogen storage characteristics of MgH2 [9,10,12,13,16–19,24,25]. Yana et al. [26] investigated the hydrogen storage properties of Mg nanoparticles confined in carbon aerogels and observed improvement in the hydrogen desorption temperature. The size range of confined MgH2 nanoparticles was mainly in the 5–20 nm field, with a majority portion smaller than 10 nm. Hudson et al. have demonstrated the catalytic effect of Fe nanoclusters decorated graphene as an effective catalyst for improving the hydrogen sorption kinetics of MgH2 [27]. Quantum dots are zero-dimensional nanomaterials whose sizes are comparable to the size of an exciton Bohr radius [28,29]. Quantum dots are found to have more active sites and hence it shows improved performance for energy storage applications [30]. Reported studies reveal that graphene quantum dots (GQDs) exhibit catalytic effect [31] and it has been used for several applications [32–40,44]. Recently, Pandey et al. [28] have demonstrated that metal oxide quantum dots have catalytic effect on improving the hydrogen sorption kinetics of MgH2. With TiO2 quantum dots as additive, the onset desorption temperature 260 °C and a reversible storage capacity up to 6.10 wt.% H2 was obtained. Even after number of studies still the desorption temperature and kinetics of de/re-hydrogenation of MgH2 is far away from what is needed for practical application. Also, the catalyst/additive used for improving the hydrogen storage characteristics of MgH2 are often synthesis from a technique which are time consuming, not environment friendly and are not controlled technique as far as size and morphology of catalyst are concerned. Keeping the above in view to best of our knowledge we have for the first time used graphene quantum dots (GQD) as a catalyst for improving the de/re-hydrogenation properties of MgH2. Keeping the above in view to best of our knowledge we have for the first time used graphene quantum dots (GQD) as a catalyst for improving the de/re-hydrogenation properties of MgH2. There are several ways to synthesizing GQD, including lithography, colloidal solution, the epitaxy process, etc. Microwave assisted synthesis method was used for GQDs synthesis, as this method is cost effective and less time consuming. Moreover, the uniform heat conduction in microwave radiation method increases the quantum yield.

Hence, in the present investigation, we have employed the as-synthesized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a catalyst additive for improving the hydrogen storage behaviour of MgH2 and found the onset desorption temperature of 300 °C and storage capacity up to 5.1 wt.%. Several methods were used to characterize the as-synthesized GQDs and MgH2 catalyzed through GQDs. A workable strategy for the catalytic mechanism for hydrogen sorption GQDs in MgH2 has also been provided by investigating the role of GQDs for MgH2 (taking the structural, micro structural studies, and superior hydrogen storage performances into consideration).
A slightly modified version of the procedure described by Kumawat et al. [41] has been used to produce GQDs (Fig. 1 ).

About 100 g of dried mango leaf powder was mixed with 100 ml of absolute ethanol and continuously stirred for 10 h using a magnetic stirrer. Then the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to obtain a supernatant mixture. The slurry was then dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The dried powder was then mixed with 50 ml of DI water and heated in a domestic 900 W microwave oven for 10 min. The resultant extract was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and dried at 60 °C for 24 h to obtain the GQD powder. Fig. 1 shows the schematic representation of synthesis protocol adopted for obtaining GQDs.

MgH2 powder was procured from Alpha Aesar (purity 98 %, having tetragonal lattice structure with lattice parameter a = 4.517 Å and c = 3.020 Å). MgH2 was ball milled together with GQDs for 24 h using a Retsch PM-400 planetary ball miller [45–49]. The ball to powder ratio was kept at 40:1 and the ball milling speed was set at 170 rpm. Handling of samples was done in a nitrogen filled glove box (MBRAUN MB10 Compact) with H2O and O2 levels ≤1 ppm.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of sample was done using XPERT PRO (PAN analytical) diffractometer employing Cu-kα radiation having wavelength of 1.5406 Å. Transmission Electron Microscopy (TECHNAI G2 20 200 kV) [50] was used for microstructural characterization of samples. Spectroscopic analysis of sample was done using UV–Vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer) and Raman Spectra using Horiba Lab RAM HR800 Raman Spectrometer. FTIR spectral analysis between wave numbers ranging from 500 to 4000 cm−1 was carried out using a PerkinElmer (spectrum 100) in transmission mode. The FEI Quanta 200 scanning electron microscope (SEM) was used for microstructural characterization. Bruker Mutlimode 8 AFM was used to take images with an atomic force microscope (AFM). Casting the aqueous GQD suspensions onto a recently sliced mica surface and drying it in the air produced the specimen.

The de-/re hydrogenation analysis of sample was carried out using an automated four-channel Sievert's type apparatus (Advanced Materials Corporation, USA) [52]. Temperature programmed desorption (TPD) of sample was performed from room temperature to 500 °C at the heating rate of 5 °C/min while the amount of hydrogen desorbed from the sample was measured. Dehydrogenation and re-hydrogenation kinetics of sample was determined at 1 atm and 15 atm hydrogen pressure, respectively at 300 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and microstructural characterization of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: UV–Vis absorption Spectrum of as-synthesized GQDs shows a characteristic absorption peak at 286 nm, which correspond to the n-π* electronic transitions of carbon structure. The representative UV–Vis spectrum of GQDs is shown in Fig. 2 (a). Fig. 2 (b) shows the FTIR spectrum of as-synthesized GQDs. The FTIR spectral bands at 3370 cm−1, 3400 cm−1 and 3250 cm−1 corresponds to stretching vibrations of Hydroxyl (-OH) group was observed. The spectral peaks observed at 3130 cm−1 and 2950 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of alkenes (C-H). The stretching vibrational peaks of (CC) and (C-C) was observed at 1620 cm−1, 1455 cm−1, respectively. The vibrational peaks observed at 1300 cm−1, 1215 cm−1 and 1080 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of esters (C=O) and the peaks observed at 720 cm−1 and 600 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of alkyl halides (C-H).

FTIR spectrum also supports the presence of oxygen functionalities in the structure (Fig. 2(b)). Fig. 2(c) shows the X-ray diffractogram of as synthesized GQDs. The XRD results of GQDs shows a diffraction peak centred at 24°, which is analogous to the graphitic structure (002) having an interplaner spacing of 3.2 Å. This increased in interlayer spacing is due to the presence of oxygen functionalities in GQDs [42].

Raman Spectroscopy is the most efficient method to detect the defects which influence the structure of native graphitic carbon. The D-band observed at 1373 cm−1 in the Raman spectra of GQDs indicates the defects and disorder in the crystalline sp2 carbon skeleton, and the G-band appeared at 1570 cm−1 represents the graphitic domains in the structure (Fig. 2(d)). Thus, the Raman spectrum reveals that there is structural similarity between GQDs and graphene oxide which clearly demonstrate the presence of sp2 as well as sp3 hybridized carbon atoms which are liable for the defects and disorder within the carbon structure. The D-band and G-band intensity ratio (ID/IG) of GQDs is observed to be 0.93. Fig. 2(d) shows the Raman spectrum of as synthesized GQDs.

The micro-structural analysis suggests that the GQDs are of the size range from 2 to 8 nm. The corresponding TEM image is shown in Fig. 2(e). HR-TEM (Fig. 2(f)) reveals that the d-spacing between graphitic layers in GQDs is 0.34 nm, similar to what observed in XRD. During microwave synthesis carbonization of the mango leaves takes place which led to the formation of nanostructures. Fig. 2(g) represent the SAED pattern of as-synthesized GQDs which indicate the crystalline nature. The size of GQD particles are in the range of 8–12 nm.

The scanning electron microscope (SEM) was used to examine the surface morphology of the GQD. Images of the GQD surface are displayed in Fig. 2(h) and 2(i), respectively. SEM images shows the almost uniformly dispersed quantum dot particles.

Additionally, an AFM investigation was performed to verify the GQDs' largely average thickness, which was found to be ∼8 nm (Fig. 2(j–l)). The presence of nanoscale GQDs is indicated by the bright spots in the AFM images. The Gwyddion software tool was utilized to obtain the line profile measurements for this particular sample. This analysis reveals that the GQD sample has four or five layers, with an average height of 8 nm. The result is based on the height measurements taken at multiple locations covering the entire topography image range.
In Table 1 , we have given the FTIR Peak of GQD consisting of wave number and Assignment GQD [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogen storage analysis of MgH2 catalyzed by GQD
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 shows the comparative TPD profiles of ball milled MgH2, with and without GQDs (as a catalyst additive) at the heating rate of 5 ᵒC/min. When 7 wt.% GQDs was employed, the onset desorption temperature of MgH2 has been found to decrease by about 60 ᵒC. Further, increasing the GQDs concentration decreases the effective hydrogen storage capacity of MgH2. Therefore, 7 wt.% of GQDs were chosen as the optimum catalyst concentration for this study. The catalytic effect is not very significant when compared to other well-known catalysts additives like Nb2O5 and other rare-earth metals. However, GQDs has improved the dehydrogenation characteristics of MgH2. For additive-free ball milled MgH2, complete dehydrogenation was achieved when the temperature reached around 400 ᵒC, whereas that for MgH2 ball milled with 7 wt.% GQDs additives, the complete dehydrogenation was achieved at about 360 ᵒC. However, due to the dead weight of additive, the MgH2 with additive exhibit a gravimetric hydrogen capacity of 5.1 wt.% H2. Whereas, the additive-free ball milled MgH2 exhibit the storage capacity of 6.2 wt.% H2. Though, the theoretical hydrogen content of MgH2 is 7.6 wt.%, the investigated ball milled MgH2 sample possess a maximum hydrogen content of 6.2 wt.%. This suggests that the MgH2 sample might have lost some of its hydrogen content during ball milling.

The dehydrogenation and rehydrogenation kinetic curves of ball milled MgH2 with 7 wt.% GQDs are shown in Fig. 4 (i) and Fig. 4(ii), respectively. The dehydrogenation kinetic measurement was carried at 300 ᵒC and 1 atm hydrogen pressure. It has been observed that, the 7 wt.% GQDs added ball milled MgH2 sample desorbed ∼5 wt.% H2 in 10 min while under same comdition the prisirne MgH2 does not dehydrogenate at all. After complete dehydrogenation, the rehydrogenation was performed at 300 ᵒC and 15 atm hydrogen pressure. It has been observed that during re-hydrogenation, the sample absorbed 3.5 wt.% H2 in less than 4 min and nearly 5 wt.% H2 in 20 min.

In order to understand the enhancement in cyclic stability of MgH2 with catalyst additive (GQDs) during de/re-hydrogenation cycles, we have performed continuous 25 de/re-hydrogenation cycles of GQDs catalyzed ball milled MgH2 sample at 300 ᵒC. The corresponding ultimate hydrogen release/uptake capacity during 25 de/re-hydrogenation cycles is shown in Fig. 5 .

From Fig. 5, it has been inferred that there is not much change in the hydrogen release/uptake capacity of MgH2 after 25 de-/re-hydrogenation cycles with GQDs as a catalyst additive. When compared with the first re-hydrogenation capacity of 5.15 wt.%, after 25 cycles, the GQDs catalyzed MgH2 sample has absorbed 4.91 wt.% H2.

The formation enthalpy of de-hydrogenated ball milled MgH2 with 7 wt.% GQDs as additive has been estimated from the van't Hoff plot derived from the pressure composition isotherms (PCI) at different temperatures. The corresponding PCI curve at different temperatures and the van't Haff plot is shown in Fig. 6 (a) and Fig. 6(b), respectively. The PCI absorption were determined at 280, 300 and 320 ᵒC.

From the slope of van't Haff plot, the enthalpy of formation of MgH2 with GQD additive is found to be 58 kJmol−1. However, for MgH2 the enthalpy of formation is reported to be 74 kJmol−1 [43]. Hence, the present study suggest an enthalpy change of ∼16 kJmol−1 for MgH2 ball milled with 7 wt.% of GQDs as additive. This change in enthalpy might be due to the effect of ball milling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tentative catalytic mechanism of GQDs in MgH2
ce:section.ce:para: The tentative catalytic mechanism of GQD can be understood with help of structural, micro structural and spectroscopic analysis of the samples. The Raman spectra of GQD are shown in Fig. 2 (d). In Fig. 2(d), the D-band observed at 1373 cm−1 clearly indicates the presence of significant number of disorder/defects in GQDs. These defects play a major role in enhancing re/de-hydrogenation kinetics of the MgH2 [2]. The flow of electrons to and from GQDs (enhanced due to defects) is the factor that will be important for the catalytic effects in enhancing the de-/re-hydrogenation behaviour of MgH2. Furthermore, since GQDs have discrete energy levels, the electron transfer will occur from solitary energy that corresponds to distinct energy levels. The combination of intrinsic defects in GQDs and the to and fro electron transfer in solitary energy levels may be the reason for showing catalytic effect in improving the de/re-hydrogenation behaviour of MgH2 [2]. The schematic illustration showing the lowering of activation energy barrier for the dissociation of MgH2 with the use of GQDs and its catalytic effect during de−/re-hydrogenation cycles is given in Fig. 7 .

The flow of electrons to and from the defects in GQDs is factor that will be important for the catalytic effect in enhancing the de/re-hydrogenation behaviour of MgH2. Furthermore, since GQDs have discrete energy levels like an atom, the electron transfer will occur from solitary energy that corresponds to distinct energy levels. The combination of intrinsic defects in GQDs and the to and fro electron transfer in solitary energy levels may be the reason for showing catalytic effect in improving the de/re-hydrogenation behaviour of MgH2.

It has been observed that GQDs as an additive enhances the de/re-hydrogenation characteristics of MgH2. When compared to additive-free ball milled MgH2, the 7 wt.% GQDs added ball milled MgH2 (7 % GQDs@MgH2) has nearly 60 ᵒC lower onset hydrogen desorption temperature. The 7 % GQDs@MgH2 also shows improved dehydrogenation and rehydrogenation kinetics with good de-/re-hydrogenation cyclic stability. After 25 cycles of de/re-hydrogenation, the 7 % GQDs@MgH2 sample shows less than 5 % loss in hydrogen capacity when compared to its initial cycle. The formation enthalpy of 7% GQDs@MgH2 sample was estimated to be 58 kJmol−1, which is 16 kJmol−1 lower than that of pristine MgH2. From the present study, it has been concluded that graphene quantum dots can act as a catalyst for improving the de-/re-hydrogenation kinetic behaviour of MgH2. The combination of intrinsic defects in GQDs and the to and fro electron transfer in solitary energy levels could be the reason for showing catalytic effect in improving the de/re-hydrogenation behaviour of MgH2.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE), New Delhi, India under the Mission Mode Hydrogen Storage Project Scheme, Department of Science and Technology, New Delhi,India and National Academy of Sciences India (NASI), are acknowledged. Thanks are also due to Late Prof. O. N. Sirvastava for his guidence and suggestions. Thanks to Prof. R. S. Tiwari for helpful discussions. The authors MAS and AB would like to acknowledge CRG-SERB (CRG/2023/003055) for financial support.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High reversible capacity hydrogen storage through Nano-LiBH4 + Nano-MgH2 system
